revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Shinto shrine]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/24598347618|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
[[commons:Category:Amanoiwatate-jinja]][[ja:天石立神社]] {{Expand Japanese|天石立神社}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Amanoiwatate Shrine | native_name = 天石立神社 | image = Amanoiwatate-jinja05s5s3200.jpg | mapframe = yes | mapframe-zoom = 15 | mapframe-wikidata = yes | mapframe-point = none | map_type = Japan | map_caption = Amanoiwatate Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|34.726708|135.959422}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato-wake no Kami|lt=Ama-no-Iwato-wake no Kami|ja|天石門別神|zh|天石門別神|qid=Q10940542|1=Ama-no-Iwato-wake no Kami}} }} {{nihongo|'''Amanoiwatate Shrine'''|天石立神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Yamato Province]]. == instance of (P31) == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|lt=Shinto shrine|ar|جينجا|arz|ضريح شنتو|bcl|Shinto shrine|ca|Jinja|cs|Šintoistická svatyně|da|Shinto-tempel|de|Shintō-Schrein|el|Σιντοϊστικό Ιερό|en|Shinto shrine|eo|Ŝintoa sanktejo|es|Jinja|eu|Jinja|fa|معبد شینتویی|fi|Šintolainen pyhäkkö|fr|Sanctuaire shinto|hi|शिंतो मंदिर|hr|Šintoistički hram|hu|Sintó szentély|id|Jinja|it|Jinja|ja|神社|ka|ძინძია|ko|신사 (신토)|lb|Shintō-Schräin|mr|शिंतो मंदिर|nl|Jinja (heiligdom)|no|Jinja|or|ଜିଞ୍ଜା|pl|Jinja|pt|Templo xintoísta|ru|Дзиндзя|sc|Jinja|simple|Shinto shrine|sl|Šintoistično svetišče|sr|Шинтоистички храм|sv|Jinja|th|ศาลเจ้าชินโต|tr|Şinto tapınağı|uk|Святилище (синто)|vi|Thần xã|wuu|神社|zh_yue|神社|zh|神社|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} * {{ill|Shikinaisha|lt=Shikinaisha|en|Shikinaisha|zh|式內社|qid=Q134917286|1=Shikinaisha}} * {{ill|Shikinai Shosha|lt=Shikinai Shōsha|en|Shikinai Shosha|ja|式内小社|simple|Shikinai Shosha|qid=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}} * {{ill|Kanpei-sha|lt=Kanpei-sha|simple|Kanpei-sha (Engishiki Jinmyocho)|qid=Q135160338|1=Kanpei-sha}}Kokugakuin University Shrine database * {{ill|Shikinai Hiteisha|lt=Shikinai Hiteisha|ja|比定社|qid=Q135026601|1=Shikinai Hiteisha}} == part of (P361) == * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Yamato Province|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Yamato Province|ja|大和国の式内社一覧|qid=Q11433427|1=List of Shikinaisha in Yamato Province}} ** Series ordinal: 24 ** P155: {{ill|Ihotachino Shrine|lt=Ihotachino Shrine|qid=Q135038844|1=Ihotachino Shrine}} ** P156: {{ill|Q11584205|ltq=Q11584205|qid=Q11584205|1=Yata ni Imasu Kushitamahiko Shrine}} == located in the administrative territorial entity (P131) == * {{ill|Yagyū-chō|lt=Yagyū-chō|qid=Q8046770|1=Yagyū-chō}} * {{ill|Q907495|ltq=Q907495|qid=Q907495|1=Yamato Province}} * {{ill|Soekami District, Nara|lt=Soekami district|en|Soekami District, Nara|ja|添上郡|ko|소에카미군|pt|Distrito de Soekami (Nara)|zh|添上郡|qid=Q7553491|1=Soekami District, Nara}} == coordinate location (P625) == * {{coord|34.726708|135.959422}} == image (P18) == * Amanoiwatate-jinja05s5s3200.jpg == dedicated to (P825) == * {{ill|Q10940542|ltq=Q10940542|qid=Q10940542|1=Ama-no-Iwato-wake no Kami}} == Kokugakuin University Digital Museum entry ID (P13677) == * 180683 ** P3831: {{ill|Kokugakuin University Shrine database|lt=Kokugakuin University Shrine database|qid=Q135159299|1=Kokugakuin University Shrine database}} == modern shrine ranking (P13723) == * {{ill|Son-sha|lt=Son-sha|ja|村社|qid=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} ** End time: {'time': '+1946-02-02T00:00:00Z', 'timezone': 0, 'before': 0, 'after': 0, 'precision': 11, 'calendarmodel': 'http://www.wikidata.org/entity/Q1985727'} ** P1534: {{ill|Q3029647|ltq=Q3029647|qid=Q3029647|1=Shinto Directive}} == country (P17) == * {{ill|Japan|lt=Japan|ab|Иапониа|ace|Jeupang|ady|Японие|af|Japan|als|Japan|alt|Јопон|ami|Ripon|am|ጃፓን|ang|Iapan|anp|जापान|an|Chapón|arc|ܝܦܢ|ar|اليابان|arnews|تصنيف:اليابان|arquote|اليابان|ary|الجاپون|arz|اليابان|ast|Xapón|as|জাপান|atj|Japan|avk|Nipona|av|Япония|awa|जापान|ay|Nihun|azb|ژاپون|az|Yaponiya|azquote|Yaponiya|ban|Jepang|bar|Japan|bat_smg|Japuonėjė|ba|Япония|bbc|Japang|bcl|Hapon|bdr|Gipun|be_x_old|Японія|be|Японія|bew|Jepang|bg|Япония|bgnews|Категория:Япония|bh|जापान|bi|Japan|bjn|Japang|blk|ဂျပန်ခမ်းထီ|bm|Japan|bn|জাপান|bnquote|জাপান|bnvoyage|জাপান|bo|ཉི་ཧོང་།|bpy|জাপান|br|Japan|bs|Japan|bsnews|Kategorija:Japan|btm|Jepang|bug|ᨍᨛᨄ|bxr|Япон Улас|ca|Japó|canews|Categoria:Japó|caquote|Japó|cbk_zam|Japón|cdo|Nĭk-buōng|ceb|Hapon|ce|Ипнойчоь|chr|ᏣᏩᏂᏏ|ch|Chapan|chy|Japan|ckb|ژاپۆن|co|Giappone|crh|Japon|csb|Japòńskô|cs|Japonsko|csnews|Kategorie:Japonsko|csquote|Japonsko|csvoyage|Japonsko|cu|Ꙗпѡнїꙗ|cv|Япони|cy|Japan|dag|Japan|da|Japan|de|Japan|denews|Kategorie:Japan|dequote|Japan|devoyage|Japan|din|Japan|diq|Japonya|dsb|Japańska|dtp|Jepun|dty|जापान|dv|ޖަޕާނު|dz|ཇ་པཱན|ee|Japan|el|Ιαπωνία|elnews|Κατηγορία:Ιαπωνία|elquote|Ιαπωνία|elvoyage|Ιαπωνία|eml|Giapòṅ|en|Japan|ennews|Category:Japan|enquote|Japan|envoyage|Japan|eo|Japanio|eonews|Kategorio:Japanujo|eoquote|Japanio|eovoyage|Japanio|es|Japón|esnews|Categoría:Japón|esquote|Japón|esvoyage|Japón|et|Jaapan|etquote|Jaapan|eu|Japonia|ext|Japón|fa|ژاپن|fanews|رده:ژاپن|faquote|ژاپن|favoyage|ژاپن|ff|Japan|fiu_vro|Jaapan|fi|Japani|finews|Luokka:Japani|fivoyage|Japani|fj|Japani|fo|Japan|frp|Japon|frr|Jaapan|fr|Japon|frnews|Catégorie:Japon|frquote|Japon|frvoyage|Japon|fur|Gjapon|fy|Japan|gag|Yaponiya|gan|日本|ga|An Bikéyah|ny|Japan|oc|Japon|olo|Japounii|om|Jaappaan|or|ଜାପାନ|os|Япон|pag|Hapon|pam|Hapon|pap|Hapon|pa|ਜਪਾਨ|pcd|Japon|pcm|Japan|pdc|Japan|pfl|Japan|pl|Japonia|plnews|Kategoria:Japonia|plquote|Japonia|plvoyage|Japonia|pms|Giapon|pnb|جاپان|pnt|Ιαπωνία|ps|جاپان|pt|Japão|ptnews|Categoria:Japão|ptquote|Japão|ptvoyage|Japão|pwn|Dripun|qu|Hapun|rm|Giapun|rmy|Japan|rn|Yapon|roa_rup|Japonia|roa_tara|Giappone|ro|Japonia|ronews|Categorie:Japonia|rovoyage|Japonia|rue|Японія|ru|Япония|runews|Категория:Япония|ruvoyage|Япония|rw|Ubuyapani|sah|Дьоппуон|sat|ᱡᱟᱯᱟᱱ|sa|जपान्|scn|Giappuni|sco|Japan|sc|Giapone|sd|جاپان|se|Jáhpan|sg|Zapöo|shn|မိူင်းၵျႃႇပၢၼ်ႇ|sh|Japan|simple|Japan|si|ජපානය|skr|جاپان|sk|Japonsko|sl|Japonska|smn|Jaapaan|sm|Iapani|sn|Japani|so|Jabaan|sq|Japonia|sqnews|Kategoria:Japoni|srn|Japan|sr|Јапан|srnews|Категорија:Јапан|srquote|Јапан|ss|IJapani|stq|Japan|st|Japane|su|Jepang|sv|Japan|svnews|Kategori:Japan|svvoyage|Japan|sw|Japani|syl|ꠎꠣꠙꠣꠘ|szl|Japůńijo|szy|Zipun|ta|யப்பான்|tanews|பகுப்பு:ஜப்பான்|tay|Gipun|tcy|ಜಪಾನ್|tdd|ᥛᥫᥒᥰ ᥓᥣᥱ ᥙᥣᥢᥱ|tet|Japaun|te|జపాన్|tg|Ҷопон|th|ประเทศญี่ปุ่น|thnews|หมวดหมู่:ประเทศญี่ปุ่น|tig|ጃፓን|ti|ጃፓን|tk|Ýaponiýa|tl|Hapon|tly|Japonijə|tn|Japan|tok|ma Nijon|to|Siapani|tpi|Siapan|trv|Tanah Tunux|tr|Japonya|trnews|Kategori:Japonya|trquote|Japonya|trvoyage|Japonya|ts|Japani|tt|Япония|tum|Japan|tw|Japan|tyv|Япония|ty|Tāpōnē|udm|Япония|ug|ياپونىيە|uk|Японія|uknews|Категорія:Японія|ukquote|Японія|ukvoyage|Японія|ur|جاپان|uz|Yaponiya|vec|Japon|vep|Japonii|ve|Japan|vi|Nhật Bản|vibooks|Các quốc gia/Nhật Bản|vivoyage|Nhật Bản|vls|Japan|vo|Yapän|war|Hapon|wa|Djapon|wo|Sapoŋ|wuu|日本|xal|Ниxуудин Нутг|xh|IJapani|xmf|იაპონია|yi|יאפאן|yo|Japan|za|Nyiedbonj|zea|Japan|zgh|ⵏⵉⴱⵓⵏ|zh_classical|日本|zh_min_nan|Ji̍t-pún|zh_yue|日本|zh|日本|zhnews|Category:日本|zhquote|日本|zhvoyage|日本|zu|IJaphani|qid=Q17|1=Japan}} == name in kana (P1814) == * あまのいわたてじんじゃ * -ノイハタチノ == TripAdvisor ID (P3134) == * 12403502 == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 4150005000035 == Commons category (P373) == * Amanoiwatate-jinja == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '奈良県奈良市柳生町柳生字岩戸谷789', 'language': 'ja'} * {'text': '奈良県奈良市柳生町柳生岩戸谷', 'language': 'ja'} == image of entrance (P9721) == * Amanoiwatate-jinja01s5s4592.jpg {{commons category|Category:Amanoiwatate-jinja}} [[JA:天石立神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11443184}} == Japanese content == {{Special characters}} {{Shrine |Name = Amanoiwatate Shrine |Image = [[Image:Amanoiwatate-jinja05s5s3200.jpg|270px]]
Worship hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=10|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|34|43|36.1|N|135|57|33.9|E}}}} |Location = {{ill|lt=Nara Prefecture|Q131287|qid=Q131287|1=Nara Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Nara City|Q169134|qid=Q169134|1=Nara (city)}} Yagyū-chō, Yagyū Aza Iwatodani 789 |Position = {{Wiki coordinates 2-stage degrees minutes seconds|34|43|36.1|N|135|57|33.9|E|region:JP-29_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Toyoiwamado no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kushiiwamado no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amano-Iwato-wake no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|qid=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}} |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} · Former {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|qid=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |Founded = |Main hall = None |Alternate name = |Pilgrimage designation etc. = |Annual festival = July 28 |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Nara|lat_deg=34|lat_min=43|lat_sec=37.6|lon_deg=135|lon_min=57|lon_sec=33|label=Amanoiwatate Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} }} '''Amanoiwatate Shrine''' (Amano-iwatate Jinja) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in the Iwatodani area of Yagyū-chō, {{ill|lt=Nara City|Q169134|qid=Q169134|1=Nara (city)}}, {{ill|lt=Nara Prefecture|Q131287|qid=Q131287|1=Nara Prefecture}}. It is situated in the mountains at an elevation of 330 {{ill|lt=meters|Q11573|qid=Q11573|1=Metre}}, on the northern foot of a small hill called Toiwazan. The shrine has no {{ill|lt=main hall|Q2044994|qid=Q2044994|1=Honden}}; instead, it enshrines and directly worships a massive rock at which it is believed to reside. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} listed in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}' as “'''Amano no Iwatate Shrine''',” and its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|qid=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. It is said to have been a place of training for the {{ill|lt=Yagyū clan|Q2975395|qid=Q2975395|lt=Yagyū family|1=Yagyū clan}} {{Cite web|Japanese book|url=https://narashikanko.or.jp/wp-content/themes/nara-portal/pdf/pamphlet_pdf/yagyukaido.pdf|title=Yagyū Kaidō Walking Guidebook|date=|publisher=Nara City Nara Brand Promotion Division|accessdate=2022-09-11}}. The area around the shrine is called Toiwadani and is a scenic spot where giant rocks and boulders are piled up one after another, including the “Ittō-seki (One-Cut Stone),” which is said to have inspired {{ill|lt=Demon Slayer: Kimetsu no Yaiba|Q24862683|qid=Q24862683|1=Demon Slayer: Kimetsu no Yaiba}}. It was counted as one of the “Ten Scenic Views of Yagyū” by {{ill|lt=Takuan Sōhō|Q632866|qid=Q632866|lt=Takuan|1=Takuan Sōhō}}, and was named “Mannenkei” ‘History of Nara Prefecture’, Vol. 13.. === Enshrined deities === {{Right| [[File:Amanoiwatate-jinja03s6s4592.jpg|thumb|none|200px|Mae-tate-iwa (left) and Ushiro-tate-iwa]] [[File:Amanoiwatate-jinja06s6s4592.jpg|thumb|none|200px|Mae-fuse-iwa (left) and Mae-tate-iwa]] [[File:Amanoiwatate-jinja07s5s4592.jpg|thumb|none|200px|Kinchaku Rock (left) and Mae-fuse-iwa]] }} Around three massive {{ill|lt=granite|Q41177|qid=Q41177|1=Granite}} rocks split into three door-like parts—“Mae-fuse-iwa,” “Mae-tate-iwa,” and “Ushiro-tate-iwa”—{{ill|lt=shimenawa|Q1887421|qid=Q1887421|1=Shimenawa}} ropes are strung through the trees, and these rocks are enshrined as {{ill|lt=shintai|Q327532|qid=Q327532|1=Shintai}} (sacred bodies). “Mae-tate-iwa” is also called “Kanbe (Kambe) Rock,” and together with the other two rocks is said to be a door stone that fell from the {{ill|lt=Amano-Iwato|Q3231835|qid=Q3231835|1=Amano-Iwato}}. In addition, a round rock called “Kinchaku Rock,” which fell from the southwestern cliff in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|qid=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 28 ({{ill|lt=1953|Q18585|qid=Q18585|1=1953}}), is also enshrined. ;Mae-tate-iwa :The shintai rock of {{ill|lt=Toyoiwamado no Kami|UNKNOWN|lt=Toyoiwamado no Mikoto}}, also called “Amano-Iwatachi Shrine.” Plate-shaped; height 6.0 m, width 7.3 m, thickness 1.2 m. ;Ushiro-tate-iwa :The shintai rock of {{ill|lt=Kushiiwamado no Kami|UNKNOWN|lt=Kushiiwamado no Mikoto}}, also called “Amano-Iwasui Shrine.” Plate-shaped. ;Mae-fuse-iwa :The shintai rock of {{ill|lt=Amano-Iwato-wake no Mikoto|UNKNOWN|lt=Amano-Iwato-wake no Mikoto}}, also called “Amatate Shrine.” Plate-shaped; height 1.2 m, width approx. 7 m, thickness 1.2 m. ;Kinchaku Rock :The shintai rock of '''Amaterasu Ōhirume no Mikoto''' ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu|qid=Q455602}}), also called “Hyūga Shrine.” Round-shaped; width 7 m, height 7 m. The enshrined deities of each rock are based on ‘Gyokuei Shūi’, and the site is noted as an excellent example of ancient {{ill|lt=Japan|Q17|qid=Q17|1=Japan}}’s {{ill|lt=nature worship|Q8189650|qid=Q8189650|1=Nature worship}}, in which giant stones were regarded as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=divine spirits|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=yorishiro|1=UNKNOWN}} ‘History of Nara Prefecture’, Vol. 5. ‘Place Names of Nara Prefecture’.. It is also said that each of the giant stones distributed throughout the entire area of the shrine is inhabited by all 3,123 deities. === Origins === This is an ancient shrine that preserves a primordial form of ritual worship in which the giant rocks themselves are revered as shintai, with no main hall, and the date of its founding is unknown ‘Shikinaisha Research Report’.. Giant rocks can be seen everywhere extending toward Mount Toiwa behind the shrine, and in the mountainous areas of Nara City, including Yagyū, there are many traces of faith centered on giant rocks and boulders. For example, Tateban Shrine, a {{ill|lt=subsidiary shrine|Q135009904|qid=Q135009904|1=Sessha}} of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yashifuyamaguchi Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} in Ōyagyū-chō, is also built atop a giant rock. In the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|qid=Q193292|1=Heian period}}, the ‘Engishiki Jinmyōchō’ lists it as a minor shrine, and according to shrine tradition, it was founded in ancient times based on the legend of the Amano-Iwato. That is, when {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tajikarao no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} opened the Amano-Iwato, the door stone is said to have flown to this place, and from the meaning “the door of the divine dwelling,” it came to be called “Kanbe Rock” ‘Ganyōki’ (a work by shrine priests of Kasuga Taisha in the early modern period) records a similar tradition, stating that the Ise Great Deity is enshrined in the center, Kasuga Daimyōjin on the left, and Tajikarao Myōjin on the right.. The area was later donated as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shrine land|1=UNKNOWN}} to {{ill|lt=Kasuga Taisha|Q714559|qid=Q714559|1=Kasuga-taisha}} during the time of {{ill|lt=Regent|Q1199966|qid=Q1199966|1=kampaku}} {{ill|lt=Fujiwara no Yorimichi|Q1370620|qid=Q1370620|1=Fujiwara no Yorimichi}}, and it is said that Kanbe Rock resounded at that time. Until the early modern period, it reportedly continued to resound whenever there was an auspicious event in the {{ill|lt=Imperial family|Q909452|qid=Q909452|1=Imperial House of Japan}} ‘Kanbe Rock Origins’.. It is said that {{ill|lt=Yagyū Muneyoshi|Q724287|qid=Q724287|1=Yagyū Munetoshi}} (Yagyū Sekishūsai) practiced swordsmanship at this shrine, and during the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, it was revered by successive lords of the {{ill|lt=Yagyū Domain|Q10863542|qid=Q10863542|1=Yagyū Domain}}. Stone lanterns inscribed with {{ill|lt=Hōei|Q1145262|qid=Q1145262|1=Hōei}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1705|Q6830|qid=Q6830|1=1705}}), donated by Yagyū Munehiro ({{ill|lt=Yagyū Toshikata|Q11534512|qid=Q11534512|lt=Toshikata|1=Yagyū Toshikata}}), and those inscribed with {{ill|lt=Kanpō|Q1145798|qid=Q1145798|1=Kanpō}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1742|Q7575|qid=Q7575|1=1742}}) by {{ill|lt=Yagyū Toshihira|Q10729347|qid=Q10729347|lt=Toshihira|1=Yagyū Toshihira}} remain. At {{ill|lt=Hōtoku-ji|Q11615783|qid=Q11615783|1=Hōtoku-ji Temple}}, the {{ill|lt=family temple|Q1084697|qid=Q1084697|1=Bodaiji}} of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yagyū family|1=UNKNOWN}}, there is preserved an origin text of this shrine, ‘Kanbe Rock Origins,’ with a {{ill|lt=colophon|Q1084697|qid=Q1084697|1=Bodaiji}} dated {{ill|lt=Kan’ei|Q1192913|qid=Q1192913|1=Kan'ei}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1625|Q6826|qid=Q6826|1=1625}}). In the {{ill|lt=Meiji period|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}, it was ranked as a village shrine, but in the ‘{{ill|lt=Shrine Register|Q11590704|qid=Q11590704|1=jinjameisaichou}}’ of the same year 7 ({{ill|lt=1874|Q7761|qid=Q7761|1=1874}}), it was listed as “Toiwadani Shrine.” === Rituals === There is a custom called “Mawari Kannushi,” in which the role of {{ill|lt=Shinto priest|Q15888735|qid=Q15888735|1=Kannushi (disambiguation)}} is taken in rotation among the districts of Yagyū-chō. The person serving as priest is responsible for replacing the shimenawa on the shintai rocks at {{ill|lt=New Year|Q701398|qid=Q701398|1=Japanese New Year}}, but during ordinary times, the {{ill|lt=miyaza|Q11454763|qid=Q11454763|1=miyaza}} of {{ill|lt=Yasaka Shrine (Yagyū-chō, Nara City)|UNKNOWN|lt=Yasaka Shrine}} takes care of the shrine ‘Gods of Japan’.. === Shrine buildings === As mentioned above, the shrine has no main hall; only a simple tiled-roof worship hall stands to the east of the shintai rocks, beside Kinchaku Rock. Between the shintai rocks and the worship hall stand two stone lanterns donated by the lords of the Yagyū Domain. In the past, a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Noh stage|1=UNKNOWN}} built by Munehiro (Toshikata) in Hōei 2 was also installed, but it has since been relocated to Yasaka Shrine in Yagyū-chō and is used in place of that shrine’s worship hall. === Ittō-seki (One-Cut Stone) === [[File:Amanoiwatate-jinja10s5s3200.jpg|thumb|200px|Ittō-seki]] About 100 meters north of the shrine lies a granite rock measuring 8 m in length, 7 m in width, and 2 m in height, which is split diagonally in a straight line near the center. According to legend, after losing a match to {{ill|lt=Kamiizumi Nobutsuna|Q1375744|qid=Q1375744|1=Kamiizumi Nobutsuna}}, Sekishūsai Muneyoshi trained in swordsmanship here every night for three years against {{ill|lt=tengu|Q742360|qid=Q742360|1=Tengu}}. One night, believing he had cut down a tengu with a single stroke, he discovered it was actually this rock, and it is said that the footprints of the tengu remain on its surface ‘History of Nara City: Folklore Volume’. ‘History of Nara Prefecture’, Vol. 13, states that they are claw marks of a tengu.. Incidentally, through this training, Muneyoshi is said to have realized the ultimate principle of “no-sword” and became the founder of {{ill|lt=Yagyū Shinkage-ryū|Q492116|qid=Q492116|1=Yagyū Shinkage-ryū}}. === Access === *From JR {{ill|lt=Nara Station|Q1150978|qid=Q1150978|1=Nara Station}} or {{ill|lt=Kintetsu Nara Station|Q6414371|qid=Q6414371|1=Kintetsu Nara Station}}, take a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nara Kōtsū Bus|1=UNKNOWN}} bound for Yagyū / Muraji Nakamura for 50 minutes, get off at “Yagyū,” then walk 15 minutes. === Notes === {{reflist}} === References === *‘Kanbe Rock Origins’ (included in ‘Shintō Taikei: Shrine Section 5, Yamato Province’, Shintō Taikei Editorial Committee, published Shōwa 62) *‘Shikinaisha Research Report’, Vol. 2, Kyō / Kinai 2, Kōgakkan University Press, Shōwa 57 *‘Gods of Japan — Shrines and Sacred Sites’, Vol. 4 Yamato (new reprint), Hakusuisha, 2000 ISBN 978-4-560-02504-8 *‘History of Nara Prefecture’, Vol. 5 Shrines, Meicho Publishing, Heisei 1 ISBN 4-626-01335-X *‘History of Nara Prefecture’, Vol. 13 Folklore (Lower), Meicho Publishing, Shōwa 63 ISBN 4-626-01327-9 *‘History of Nara City: Shrines and Temples Volume’, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, Shōwa 60 ISBN 4-642-01549-3 *‘History of Nara City: Folklore Volume’, Nara City, Shōwa 43 *‘Kadokawa Dictionary of Japanese Place Names 29: Nara Prefecture’, Kadokawa Shoten, Heisei 2 ISBN 4-04-001290-9 *‘Place Names of Nara Prefecture’ (Japan Historical Place Names Series 30), Heibonsha, 1981 ISBN 4-58-249030-1 === Surroundings === *{{ill|lt=Hōtoku-ji|Q11615783|qid=Q11615783|1=Hōtoku-ji Temple}} *{{ill|lt=Former Yagyū Domain Chief Retainer Residence|Q11511073|qid=Q11511073|1=Kyū Yagyū-han Karō Yashiki}} *{{ill|lt=Yagyū Jinya|Q109466392|qid=Q109466392|1=Yagyū Jin'ya}} *{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Masaki-zaka Kenzen Dōjō|1=UNKNOWN}} *{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Oyamada Family Branch House|1=UNKNOWN}} === External links === Shrine]][[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamato Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamato Province]] [[Category:Son-sha in Nara Prefecture]]
